fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by and respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by ) a retract of su=_Asv.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via .
If A is n+1-truncated, then for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-truncated.
By induction on n; the base case for n=-2 is given by , and the other cases are trivial.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by and .
For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is contractible.
Immediate by identification induction.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by and respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by ) a retract of su=_Asv.

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .

Let A be n-truncated and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and . In the inductive step, we need to show that for all u,v\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type u=v is n+1-truncated assuming the same of all the identification types of A and Bx for x:A. By , u=v is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:u.1=v.1\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} p_*u.2=v.2 and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis; thus the result follows from .
Let s\colon A\to B exhibit A as a retract of B; then \mathsf {act}_{s}\colon x = y\to sx=sy exhibits x=y as a retract of sx=sy for each x,y:A.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.
